title: Context-indexed task-separated feedback candidate
notes: Seventh-round mutation of the context-indexed dynamic-feedback module. It separates production task setting from judgment task setting so unmonitored production, elicited production, correctness judgment, and editing/correction tasks are not routed through a single task_framing node. It is a scoped mutation for production/judgment divergence, not a general account.

conditioning_axes.community: Required. Interpret licensing and production relative to a specified speech community or comparison set.
conditioning_axes.norm_centre: Required when standard_language_ideology, metalinguistic_condemnation, editorial_correction_probability, or schooling_instructional_exposure are used.
conditioning_axes.genre: Required for register-sensitive production or judgment settings.
conditioning_axes.medium: Required when speech, edited prose, messaging, classroom response, or test response may diverge.
conditioning_axes.task_framing: Required for reported_acceptability and grammaticality_attribution; specify whether the judgment setting cues correctness, naturalness, category assignment, register fit, or community membership.
conditioning_axes.speaker_identity: Required when speaker cues, matched-guise manipulations, or stigmatized forms affect reported judgments.

edges.rationale: Community licensing makes a form available for production in the specified community and setting.
Unmonitored conversation, elicited production, edited prose, and test contexts can change production probability in different directions.
Production in a setting contributes to observed usage in later matching samples.
Licensing at t1 supports later production availability in matching contexts.
Correction pressure can suppress later production in monitored or school-like settings.
Instructional exposure can increase uptake of standard-language ideology for the relevant form.
Standard-language ideology can generate explicit condemnation in correctness-oriented settings.
Explicit condemnation predicts correction in school, editing, or grammar-test settings.
Correction can influence grammaticality attribution while remaining separable from community licensing.
Judgment procedures cue what kind of response is requested: correctness, naturalness, editing, category assignment, or register fit.
Judgment settings can change reported acceptability independently of production probability.
Correctness, naturalness, register-fit, and community-membership framings can shift reported acceptability.
Task framing can shift explicit grammaticality attribution by cueing the requested status category.
Acceptability reports can measure licensing, but judgment setting and framing bias the indicator.
Acceptability reports provide contextual warrant for attribution without defining grammatical status.
Community licensing is evidence for grammaticality attribution in the relevant community and task frame.
Genre determines which forms are locally appropriate for the activity type.
Medium conditions whether a form is ordinary, marked, or correction-prone.
Higher register or genre fit tends to increase reported acceptability in situated judgment tasks.
Speaker cues shape the social-indexical value assigned to a form in the judgment context.
Indexical associations can shift reported acceptability without changing community licensing.
